mappings.rationaleAI: Full history of Hinduism from Indus Valley through contemporary practice covering all major periods (A), Vedic tradition (A.3, A.3.a), Upanisads (A.3.c), devotional movements (A.5-A.10), Rig Veda as scripture (B.1.a), dharma/karma/moksha doctrines (C.1.c.ii, C.1.c.iii), Vedism/Brahmanism (C.2.a), Vaisnavism (C.2.b), modern movements (C.2.f), and caste system (C.3.c).
Six classical schools address Hindu philosophy (B.3), Upanishadic tradition (A.3.c), and karman/transmigration doctrines (C.1.c.ii, C.1.c.iii).
Comprehensive coverage of Sikhism spanning all historical periods (D.2.a.i through D.2.a.vi), religious literature (D.2.b), and beliefs and practices (D.2.c).
Buddhism as challenge to Brahmanism addresses the heterodox period (A.4).
The historical Buddha addresses the heterodox period (A.4).
Gandhi connects to modern Hinduism (A.9) and ethical/nationalist movements (C.2.f).
Contemporary India connects to Hinduism today (A.10), modern movements (C.2.f), and caste (C.3.c).
Ritual covers Hindu sacrifice and worship (C.3.a) and sacred times and places (C.3.b).
Tibetan Buddhism connects to the heterodox period (A.4).
Spirituality connects to Hindu mysticism (B.4) and the three margas (C.1.c.iii).
Islam's presence provides context for Sikhism's Islamic-Hindu background (D.2.a.i).
Religion broadly addresses the history of Hinduism (A) and its beliefs and practices (C).
Provides comparative context for understanding Eastern religious philosophy alongside the Hindu philosophical traditions of 823.B.3.
Pakistan connects to Sikhism's Islamic-Hindu background (D.2.a.i) and Sikhism in independent India and Pakistan (D.2.a.vi).
Epic connects to post-Vedic Sanskrit literature (B.1.b) and Hindu mythology (B.2).
Monasticism connects to religious orders and holy men (C.3.d).
